doc_title: TinyLlama: An Open-Source Small Language Model

items.fact: TinyLlama builds upon the architecture and tokenizer of Llama 2 (Touvron et al., 2023b)
items.evidence_span: Building on the architecture and tokenizer of Llama 2 (Touvron et al., 2023b)
TinyLlama leverages various advances contributed by the open-source community (e.g., FlashAttention (Dao, 2023))
This provides a guideline on how to optimally select the model size and allocate the amount of training data when the compute budget is fixed.
Touvron et al. (2023a) highlight the importance of the inference budget, instead of focusing solely on training compute-optimal language models.
Thaddé (2023) suggest that existing scaling laws (Hoffmann et al., 2022) may not predict accurately in situations where smaller models are trained for longer periods.

items.fact: TinyLlama uses SlimPajama (Soboleva et al., 2023) for pretraining data.
items.evidence_span: We adopt a mixture of natural language data and code data to pre-train TinyLlama, sourcing natural language data from SlimPajama (Soboleva et al., 2023)

items.fact: TinyLlama uses Starcoderdata (Li et al., 2023) for pretraining data.
items.evidence_span: code data from Starcoderdata (Li et al., 2023)

items.fact: TinyLlama adopts the Transformer architecture based on Vaswani et al. (2017)
items.evidence_span: We use a Transformer architecture based on Vaswani et al. (2017)

items.fact: TinyLlama uses RoPE (Rotary Positional Embedding) (Su et al., 2021)
items.evidence_span: We use RoPE (Rotary Positional Embedding) (Su et al., 2021)
we apply RMSNorm (Zhang and Sennrich, 2019) as our normalization technique
we follow Llama 2 and combine Swish and Gated Linear Unit together, which is referred to as SwiGLU (Shazeer, 2020)

items.fact: TinyLlama uses AdamW optimizer (Loshchilov and Hutter, 2019)
items.evidence_span: we employ an autoregressive language modeling objective during the pretraining phase. Consistent with Llama 2 's settings, we utilize the AdamW optimizer (Loshchilov and Hutter, 2019)
